label: Computational Theory of Mind
definition: The theory that the mind is a computational system and that thinking is, at bottom, the rule-governed manipulation of mental representations, providing the foundational rationale for the project of artificial intelligence.

body.text: The computational theory of mind holds that the mind is, or is realized by, a computational system: mental processes are computations, and thinking is the manipulation of internal representations according to formal rules. On this view the brain is a kind of information-processing machine, and mental states such as beliefs and desires are representational states that the machine operates on, much as a computer operates on data structures. The idea has roots in Alan Turing's formal model of computation (1936) and in Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts's 1943 demonstration that idealized neurons could compute logical functions. Its most influential modern statement is Jerry Fodor's, especially "The Language of Thought" (1975), which proposed that thinking takes place in an internal symbolic system, a "Mentalese", with its own syntax and compositional semantics. Hilary Putnam's early functionalism gave it philosophical footing by identifying mental states with the states of an abstract computing machine.

The theory's great explanatory attraction is that it promises to bridge meaning and mechanism. Mental states have content, they are about things, an instance of intentionality, and they also cause behavior. Computation is the one well-understood process that is at once formal, and so mechanically implementable, and capable of respecting semantic relations, because operations defined over the shapes of symbols can be set up to preserve truth and inferential coherence. This is sometimes put as the formality condition: the system manipulates representations by their syntactic form, yet because the syntax mirrors the semantics, the manipulations make rational sense. Fodor's classical version emphasizes a language-like medium with compositional structure, which neatly explains why thought is productive (we can entertain endlessly many novel thoughts) and systematic (anyone who can think "the dog chased the cat" can think "the cat chased the dog"). The classical computational theory is closely allied with functionalism: both abstract the mind away from its physical substrate, which is what licenses the claim that a mind could run on non-biological hardware and thus underwrites the very possibility of AI and of artificial-general-intelligence.

The theory faces sustained objections. John Searle's chinese-room argument contends that formal symbol manipulation can never amount to genuine understanding, because syntax does not suffice for semantics, which is the same gap the symbol-grounding-problem names: how the system's symbols could mean anything intrinsically rather than only to an interpreter. Jerry Fodor himself argued that central cognition, the part responsible for belief fixation and relevance, may be computationally intractable in a way that exposes the frame-problem, since computation is sensitive to the local form of representations while relevance depends on their global context. Advocates of embodied cognition argue that treating cognition as disembodied internal computation leaves out the body and the environment that make thought possible. And the classical symbolic version is challenged by connectionism and modern deep learning, whose distributed, sub-symbolic representations and learned weights do not obviously implement a language of thought, prompting debate over whether neural networks refute classical computationalism or simply realize a different kind of computation.

The computational theory of mind matters because it is the deepest theoretical justification for believing that minds can be built. If thinking just is computation of the right sort, then a machine that performs that computation is not merely simulating a mind but has one, and the engineering of intelligence becomes continuous with the science of cognition. Much of AI proceeds on this assumption even where it goes unstated. The live questions are whether the relevant computation is classical and symbolic or distributed and statistical, whether it must be embodied and grounded to carry real content, and whether computation, of any kind, can deliver not just understanding but the felt experience that the hard-problem-of-consciousness insists is a further thing. Where one stands on the computational theory of mind largely fixes how one reads the prospects and the limits of artificial intelligence.
